coredata.dc:title: Selective sensing of curcumin using L-cysteine derived blue luminescent graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sudarsanakumar, C.
Thomas, Subin
Mathew, Smitha
Arundhathi, S.
Raj, D. Rithesh
Prasanth, S.
Thomas, Riju K.
coredata.dc:description: L-cysteine, a natural amino acid, was used to prepare Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by the bottom-up approach in a one-step direct carbonization. The morphology and the fluorescent behaviour of the prepared GQDs were analyzed. Selective fluorescence-based sensing of curcumin was demonstrated using the prepared GQDs. The sensing was found to be independent of the pH of GQDs and the curcumin was detected even in its degraded form in the alkaline media. Secondary inner filter effects explicate the sensing of curcumin by the GQDs. A static cum dynamic quenching in the fluorescent intensity of GQDs was observed upon addition of curcumin. Curcumin sensing was found to be selective among a number of biomolecules and metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
L-cysteine
Fluorescence quenching
Curcumin sensing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: One of the authors, Subin Thomas, thanks Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment for the Post-Doctoral fellowship. The authors Prasanth S. and Riju K. Thomas like to acknowledge UGC for BSR-RFSMS fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of direct carbonization of L-cysteine to GQDs.

TEM and X-ray diffraction pattern for the GQDs prepared by carbonization of L-cysteine. (a) HRTEM image of the GQDs with their positions marked and the low-resolution image (inset of figure). (b) HRTEM of the GQDs with the lattice spacing values. (c) SAED pattern. (d) X-ray diffraction pattern of the GQDs.
Raman spectrum of GQDs obtained from L-cysteine carbonization.
FTIR spectra of L-cysteine and L-cysteine derived GQDs.
(a) Optical absorption spectra and (b) excitation dependent photoluminescence spectra of L-cysteine derived GQDs.

(a) GQDs emission in the visible blue region and the overlapping curcumin absorption and (b) fluorescence quenching of the GQDs by the addition of curcumin (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
The quenching of GQD fluorescence by the addition of curcumin at pH 4 (a) and at pH 9 (b) and by adding curcumin dissolved in 0.1 M NaOH (c).
Stern-Volmer plot obtained by the addition of curcumin to GQDs.
(a) Fluorescence decay curves for GQDs with the addition of different concentrations of curcumin, (b) variation in fluorescence lifetime with curcumin concentration.
Percentage selectivity of GQDs towards curcumin, few biomolecules and some metal ions.
Fluorescence lifetime parameters for GQDs and its variation with the addition of curcumin.

head.ce:title: Selective sensing of curcumin using L-cysteine derived blue luminescent graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pure and Applied Physics, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala, 686560, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pure and Applied Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: St. Dominic’s College Kanjirappally, Kottayam, Kerala, 686512, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Amrita University, Amritapuri campus, Vallikavu, Kerala, 690525, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: L-cysteine, a natural amino acid, was used to prepare Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by the bottom-up approach in a one-step direct carbonization. The morphology and the fluorescent behaviour of the prepared GQDs were analyzed. Selective fluorescence-based sensing of curcumin was demonstrated using the prepared GQDs. The sensing was found to be independent of the pH of GQDs and the curcumin was detected even in its degraded form in the alkaline media. Secondary inner filter effects explicate the sensing of curcumin by the GQDs. A static cum dynamic quenching in the fluorescent intensity of GQDs was observed upon addition of curcumin. Curcumin sensing was found to be selective among a number of biomolecules and metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a two-dimensional lattice of carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice structure with superior electrical, mechanical and chemical properties. GQDs, a sort of carbon nanodots (C-dots), have the traits of both graphene and carbon nanodots. The quantum confinement will provide additional electrical and optical properties to the graphene by the introduction of a band gap [1] and by the edge effects [2]. In comparison, the GQDs have a number of advantages over the semiconductor counterpart of quantum dots (QDs) and other carbonaceous QDs like low toxicity, improved biocompatibility and higher photo-stability. Consequently, the GQDs have wide applications in electronic/photonic devices [3–5], bio-imaging/sensing [6–8], water purification [9] and catalysis [10].

Curcumin is an organic chromogenic reagent and a chemopreventive agent. Chemically, curcumin is a diferuloylmethane molecule (1,7-bis(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenol)-1,6-heptadiene-3,5-dione) containing two ferulic acid residues joined by a methylene bridge [11]. The chemical, biological and pharmacological effect of curcumin was studied by various scientists [11–15]. It is the major component of the spice Curcuma longa, a medicinal plant known for thousands of years [16]. Its biological activities were studied using both experimental and theoretical techniques [17,18]. Curcumin was shown to interact with a number of biological molecules through amino acids and a number of biophysical tools like fluorescence quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), surface plasmon resonance, spectrophotometry, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy etc. were used to clear up the interactions [11,19].

The very first report on carbon nanodots was done by Scrivens and co-workers [20]. Since then, the work on carbonaceous quantum dots have several extensions and different application areas were developed [21]. It is important to prepare the GQDs via facile and cost-effective routes and is better to avoid toxic reagents and concentric acidic/alkaline conditions. Towards this end, bottom-up synthesis of GQDs from organic precursors is a promising route. So far, several organic precursors like citric acid [22,23], pyrene [24], l-glutamic acid [25], 1,3,5-tri-amino-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene, glucose [26–28] etc. were identified as feasible starting materials for GQDs synthesis.

In this work, L-cysteine a member of the amino acid group, is used in direct carbonization procedure to produce graphene quantum dots and a feasible method for the detection of curcumin using these GQDs is proposed. This is for the first time, L-cysteine is utilized as a precursor for the preparation of GQDs. L-cysteine was previously used with GQDs to provide chirality and for amine functionalization [29].

Analytical grade L-cysteine was purchased from Sigma Aldrich and was used as such for the preparation of GQDs. Aspartic acid (Asc), Ascorbic acid (Aa), Glycine (Gly), Phenylalanine (Phe), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Human serum albumin (HSA) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. NaOH, Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and the metal salts of Cadmium, Copper, Iron and Zinc were purchased from CDH Chemicals. Curcumin was purchased from Merck. Distilled water was used throughout for the preparations in this study.

In a typical reaction procedure, 0.5 g of L-cysteine was taken in a glass vial and heated to 215 °C in an oil bath for 30 min. After 30 min, the color of the material changed from white to brownish black, indicating the formation of GQDs (Fig. 1 ). The GQDs were then dissolved in 20 ml water under vigorous stirring at room temperature followed by filtering of the solution. The GQD solution was further diluted using water or pH buffer solutions in 1:8 ratio before measurements or sensing studies.

The X-Ray diffraction pattern of the GQDs was recorded using PANalytical X-Ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was done using JEOL JEM 2100 with a LaB6 filament at an operating voltage of 200 kV. Raman spectra of GQDs were recorded at room temperature using a confocal Raman microscope (Alpha 300 RA) equipped with 633 nm excitation radiation. Dried samples were used to record FTIR spectra and were done using Shimadzu IRPrestige-21, with ZnSe ATR crystal (Pike technologies). Jasco V-350 UV–vis spectrophotometer was used in the absorption studies. A time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer Fluoromax-4 (HoribaScientific) was used in luminescence measurements and in fluorescence lifetime determination. The lifetime measurements were carried out at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm using a picosecond diode source (Horiba Scientific Spectra-NanoLED source S-340).

Curcumin was prepared at different molar concentrations ranging from 1 μM to 200 μM in different solvents. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and 0.1 M NaOH were used to prepare the curcumin solution. Initially, the fluorescence spectrum of GQD was recorded at 350 nm excitation. Curcumin solutions having different molarities (1 ml) were added to 2 ml of GQD solution and their fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The selectivity of the proposed method was evaluated by allowing the GQDs solution to interact with different bio-molecules (Cysteine, HSA, Aspartic acid, Ascorbic acid, Glycine, Phenylalanine and BSA) and to different metal ions (Cadmium, Copper, Iron and Zinc). 1 mM analyte solutions (1 ml) were added to two ml of the GQD solution for the selectivity measurements.

Fig. 2 (a–c) shows the HRTEM images of the prepared GQDs. The GQDs have an elliptical shape with the major axis found to vary from 4 to 8 nm. The lattice planes were clearly visible with an interplanar spacing ∼2.35 Å [28]. Fig. 2(d) is the X-ray diffraction pattern for the prepared GQDs, showing a broad peak c.a. 25°, which indicates the (002) plane of carbon atoms with a shift in comparison with graphite. The oxygen functionalities present in the GQDs may caused the peak shift. The X-ray diffraction profile was similar to that of GQDs in earlier reports [25,27].

Raman spectra of GQDs is shown in Fig. 3 , which has overlapped broad D and G peaks c.a. 1370 and 1547 cm−1 respectively. The relative intensity of D to G-band (ID/IG) is 0.82, which indicates the crystalline nature of the prepared GQDs with associated disorder. The present mode of GQD preparation resulted in an ID/IG value comparable to other methods of GQD preparation [5,30].

The FTIR spectrum of the L-cysteine used for the preparation of the GQDs and the spectrum correspond to the GQDs are given in Fig. 4 . The FTIR spectroscopy points towards the formation of GQDs which can be inferred from the structural changes. The peak at 1550-1600 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of L-cysteine corresponds to the COO vibrations [31,32]. The same absorption was found in the GQDs spectrum and this indicates the presence of COOH functional groups in L-cysteine derived GQDs. The strong, broad peak at 2900–3440 cm−1 indicates NH vibrations of the NH2 group and hence evidenced the presence of NH2 functional groups with the GQDs [31]. The L-cysteine SH vibrations present at 2550-2670 cm−1 was absent in the GQD spectra [33]. The CC stretching vibration peak, which is the characteristic feature of the graphite structure exhibited an absorption maximum c.a. 1640 cm−1 [27]. The CH stretching vibrations c.a. 1360 cm−1 was because of the incomplete carbonization [22]. The peak at 1020 cm−1 can be attributed to CO stretching vibrations [27].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs is given in Fig. 5 (a). The absorption spectrum has a peak c.a. 227 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC bonds [34,35]. The broad absorption band c.a. 340 nm is from the n-π* transitions of CO bonds and the broadness of this peak indicates the variable size distribution of the GQDs as obtained from the TEM analysis [36]. The luminescence of GQDs is speculated to have its contributions from both quantum size effects and surface defects [37,38]. Excitation dependent luminescence emission was observed by varying the excitation wavelength from 320 to 420 nm (Fig. 5(b)). The emission wavelength was found to shift towards the red end from 428.5 nm to 473 nm while increasing the excitation from 320 to 420 nm. Maximum emission intensity was observed for 350 nm excitation. The excitation dependent emission was reported in GQDs as a result of the optical selection of differently sized GQDs and due to the surface defects [21,37,39]. By exciting the GQDs with 350 nm source, PL spectrum was recorded with peak at 425 nm, distinctive from the electrochemical and hydrothermally prepared GQDs and the difference can be due to the effects of size and functional groups [5,40].

From Fig. 6 (a), it is clear that the fluorescence emission spectrum of GQDs complementary overlaps with the absorption spectra of curcumin. Curcumin sensing experiments were conducted as mentioned in the materials and methods. Fig. 6(b) shows the photoluminescence spectra of GQDs (pH 7) with the addition of curcumin dissolved in DMSO with its quantity varying from 0.0 μM to 200 μM, at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Fluorescence quenching of GQDs is evident from Fig. 6(b). As the concentration of curcumin exceeded 100 μM, it began to emit together with the GQDs. The curcumin emission spectrum c.a. 495 nm is shown in Fig. 6(b). It is plausible to suggest that the curcumin quenched the GQDs fluorescence by a secondary inner filter effect. The inner filter effect by curcumin was already used in the sensing of curcumin and Fluoride ion [41,42]. The binding between GQD and the curcumin seems to be strong as indicated by the blue shift in the fluorescence spectra. The pH of GQD solution was adjusted to both 4 and 9 and the fluorescence quenching studies were performed to evaluate the effect of pH on curcumin sensing (Fig. 7 (a) and (b)) by the L-cysteine derived GQDs. The percentage of quenching, by adding 100 μM curcumin, for neutral and pH 4 GQDs were found to be 73.35 and 75.42 respectively. At alkaline pH (pH 9), the quenching percentage succeeded by a factor ≈13.5. For the neutral and acidic pH, the curcumin peak c.a. 495 nm was found to predominate at higher curcumin concentrations. In the alkaline pH curcumin

fluorescence emission was found to be absent up to 1000 μM concentration different from the acidic and neutral pH. The structural deformations occured due to hydrolytic splitting might be serving towards the fluorescence changes in the alkaline pH [43,44]. To confirm this, curcumin sensing was repeated by keeping the GQDs pH at neutral and by mixing it with curcumin dissolved in a strong alkaline medium (0.1 M NaOH) (Fig. 7(c)). The results obtained agreed with the earlier pH 9 experiments.

The quenching of GQDs florescence by curcumin can be depicted by Stern-Volmer equation
F 0
F
=
1 +
K D
Q
1 +
K S
Q
where F0 and F are fluorescence intensity in the absence of quencher and in the presence of quencher respectively at a concentration [Q]. KS is the static quenching constant and KD is the dynamic quenching constant. Fig. 8 shows F0/F as a function of curcumin concentration. The graph shows an exponential increase in the F0/F value with increasing curcumin concentration, which is an indication of the combined static and dynamic quenching [45].

Time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was used to study the fluorescence lifetime of GQDs in the absence and in the presence of curcumin at different concentrations. The fluorescence decay curves were plotted in each case (Fig. 9 (a)) and were used to determine the fluorescence lifetime using the multi-exponential equation
τ m
=
∑ i
τ i
a i
=
τ 1
a 1
+
τ 2
a 2
+
τ 3
a 3
where τ1, τ2 and τ3 are the decay times of the multi-exponential fluorescence decay. a1, a2 and a3 are the amplitudes. The fluorescence lifetime parameters were tabulated in Table 1 . The lifetime for GQDs was found to be 2.27 ns. Addition of curcumin with varying molarity from 1 to 20 μM made only minor variation to fluorescence lifetime. More pronounced variation in lifetime was observed with higher amounts of curcumin, which could be due to the combined static cum dynamic quenching.

Selectivity of the proposed sensing mechanism was tested against various biomolecules and metal ions. Fig. 10 shows the percentage of selectivity for the L-cysteine derived GQDs towards different analytes. The present study reveals a selective detection of curcumin among the various analytes using the L-cysteine derived GQDs.

A direct carbonization procedure was used to produce Graphene quantum dots from L-cysteine. The size of GQDs was found to vary from 4 to 9 nm A number of different functional groups were found attached to the defective carbon rings. The GQDs showed strong excitation dependent emission. Selective sensing of curcumin was observed with the GQDs using fluorescence quenching approach and the sensing was found to be pH independent. A combined static cum dynamic quenching mechanism was identified by the Stern-Volmer plot and further supported by the fluorescence lifetime measurements. The GQDs could selectively detect curcumin among a number of metal ions and biomolecules.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors, Subin Thomas, thanks Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment for the Post-Doctoral fellowship. The authors Prasanth S. and Riju K. Thomas like to acknowledge UGC for BSR-RFSMS fellowship.
